title: Transition Velocity: How Fast Should an Advisor Transition Actually Be?
imageAlt: Transition Velocity: How Fast Should an Advisor Transition Actually Be?
author: FastTrackr AI Team
description: The industry has accepted 90 days as normal because no one has published a better benchmark. In 2026, that's changing. LPL Financial benchmarks 45 days...
excerpt: The industry has accepted 90 days as normal because no one has published a better benchmark. In 2026, that's changing. LPL Financial benchmarks 45 days...
content:
The industry has accepted 90 days as normal because no one has published a better benchmark. In 2026, that's changing. LPL Financial benchmarks 45 days as their standard — 87% of AUM transitioned by month two. FastTrackr AI's benchmark is 21 days with full automation. The gap between these numbers isn't about resources or effort. It's about whether the process is automated end-to-end or still running on manual workflows. Here's what the data shows, and what your transition velocity target should be.

The current benchmark landscape

Three numbers define the range for advisor transition velocity in 2026.

90 days is the industry average for traditional manual transitions. According to the 2026 Broker-Dealer Transition Playbook, transitioning a book can take 3–6 months and involves thousands of forms, usually resulting in NIGOs. This is the status quo for firms running transitions on spreadsheets with manual form completion.

45 days is LPL Financial's reported benchmark — 87% of AUM transitioned by the second month. This represents a firm that has invested in transition support infrastructure. It's better than the industry average, but it's still not fast.

21 days is what AI-automated transitions deliver. FastTrackr AI's benchmark is a 75% reduction in end-to-end transition time — which takes the 90-day average to approximately 21–22 days. For ACATS transfers that take 5–6 business days when submissions are clean, a 21-day total timeline is operationally achievable when the data gathering and form completion phases are automated.

The benchmark you're held to depends on which of these your firm is operating in.

Why transition velocity is a recruiting competitive advantage

For high-volume recruiting firms, transition velocity is not an operations metric — it's a market differentiator.

When an advisor is evaluating firms, "how fast will I be fully operational?" is one of the most important questions they ask. It affects client anxiety during the move, revenue continuity, and how quickly the advisor can focus on building their practice at the new firm rather than managing the transition.

A firm that can credibly promise 21 days — and deliver it — closes more advisors than one that says "typically 60–90 days." This is especially true in competitive recruitment situations where two or three firms are pursuing the same advisor with similar compensation packages.

According to Diamond Consultants' 2025 data, 11,172 experienced advisors changed firms in 2025 — a 16% increase from the prior year, with 54 teams carrying more than $1B in AUM making moves. In a market with this volume of movement, the recruiter who can compete on speed as well as economics is working with a structural advantage.

Every day in transition is one more day for a client to change their mind. Faster transitions protect books. Faster transitions win recruitments.

How to measure transition velocity for your firm

Most firms don't have a precise transition velocity metric. They know transitions are "slow" or "went well" — but they don't track days-to-complete with the same rigor they apply to other business metrics.

Building the metric requires three data points per transition:

  1. Start date — the day the advisor signs with the new firm and data gathering begins

  2. First submission date — the day the first custodian forms are submitted

  3. Completion date — the day the last account transfer clears

The gap between start date and completion date is transition velocity. Track it per transition, average it quarterly, and break it down by cause when it's slower than target: data gathering delay, NIGO corrections, or custodial queue time.

Once you have the metric, improvement is targeted. If data gathering is the bottleneck, automation there moves the needle most. If NIGO corrections dominate, pre-submission validation is the fix. If custodial queue time is the primary variable, parallel submission across custodians is the lever.

What 21 days looks like operationally

A 21-day transition isn't a compressed version of a 90-day one — it's a structurally different process.

Week 1: AI platform extracts and maps client data from source systems. 2–3 days instead of 2–4 weeks. Account mapping, custodian requirements, and pre-submission validation all run in parallel.

Week 2: Pre-validated forms submitted to custodians with under 5% NIGO rate. Real-time tracking active across all accounts. Every stakeholder — advisor, transition team, recruiter — sees live status.

Week 3: ACATS transfers complete. Client communication wraps. The advisor is fully operational, managing client conversations rather than paperwork corrections.

This isn't theoretical. According to data from the Terrana Group's 2026 analysis, the pipeline of advisors transitioning from wirehouses to independent RIAs remains robust entering 2026 — and the firms capturing those moves are the ones that have solved the operational problem.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is considered a fast advisor transition in 2026?

In 2026, a 45-day transition is the midpoint benchmark (LPL's standard), 30 days is a performance target, and 21 days is achievable with full end-to-end automation. The 90-day industry average represents transitions still running on manual processes. Anything under 30 days requires automated data extraction, AI-powered form pre-population, and pre-submission NIGO validation.

What is the benchmark for transition velocity for high-volume recruiting firms?

The competitive benchmark for high-volume recruiting firms is 30 days or fewer, with 21 days as the performance standard for fully automated operations. Firms that consistently deliver under 30 days have a measurable recruiting advantage over those quoting 60–90 day timelines to advisors evaluating their move.

How long should a $100M AUM transition take?

A $100M AUM transition should take 21–30 days with full automation. The AUM size doesn't significantly affect the timeline — what matters is the number of accounts, the number of custodians involved, and whether the process is automated or manual. A 100-account transition at $100M can complete in 3 weeks with clean, automated submissions.

What factors most affect transition speed?

The three biggest factors are data gathering time (manual: 2–4 weeks; automated: 2–3 days), NIGO rate on form submissions (manual: 15–20%; automated: under 5%), and whether multiple transition phases run in parallel or sequentially. Together, eliminating these three delays accounts for the difference between a 90-day and a 21-day transition.

How do you measure transition velocity across multiple concurrent moves?

Track start date, first submission date, and completion date for every transition. Average time-to-complete per quarter. Break down variance by cause: data gathering delay, NIGO correction cycles, or custodial queue time. A real-time dashboard that tracks all concurrent transitions provides this visibility automatically — manual tracking across multiple moves is itself a source of delay.

What is the relationship between transition speed and client retention?

Transition speed directly affects client retention during the move. Every additional day the advisor isn't fully operational is a day that clients are anxious, accounts aren't transferring, and competitors have an open window. FastTrackr AI's analysis estimates $19B in annual asset loss from slow advisor transitions — driven largely by client attrition during extended timelines. Faster transitions protect books.

How does automation improve transition velocity for high-volume recruiters?

Automation improves velocity at three points: data extraction (2–4 weeks → 2–3 days), form completion and submission (15–20% NIGO → under 5%), and tracking (manual status calls → real-time dashboard). For high-volume recruiters managing 10–20 concurrent transitions, these improvements don't just speed up individual moves — they make it possible to maintain quality and visibility across all of them simultaneously.

Set a velocity target. Measure against it. Close the gap. In a market where 11,000+ experienced advisors are moving every year, the firm that moves them fastest wins more of them.
